speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Manya Winshell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We left off talking about when you realized you had to leave. And that year was--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What special events occurred prior to 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was OK because we got used to the life in Soviet Union. And in 1941, that was the bad time because the Germans started the war with the Soviet Union. And they started June 22, 1941. And June 26, that's a few days after, I escaped with my baby and my husband. We were very lucky that we could escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when-- what year did you have a baby? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, You were how old at that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bob. Bobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what were the conditions? Now you had a baby, and what were-- what were the conditions now that you had a child? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard because when the war broke out, I wanted to escape. So and I was 24 years old, full of energy, and my husband was 29. So we took the baby and we ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you had the baby a year before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year into the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year into the war. Now you have a child. Now you have a brand new child. You leave. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked from town to town. We got into--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked. OK, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. My husband and I, so we had to carry the baby. And we took some things, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some clothes for the baby. And whatever I had that's worth something, like a watch, a bracelet or things that-- that were important that I could sell someplace, we took with us. And that's all. And we went from town to town [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until we got into-- yeah, we crossed the border, the Soviet Union. It was the Soviet Union, but there was still a border from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we crossed. And the-- the soldiers-- how could we do it, because the army, the Soviet Army, was pulling back because the Germans were very close. And they took city and city after city. It didn't take long then. And there was bombing all over. And they didn't stop us. The Russian Army, I mean the Soviet Union Army, they let us go. They wouldn't stop us. They were pulling with the tanks and everything, and they let us go, too. That, I'll never forget, how good they were. And then we came to-- to a town that had that a train already, that we could go on a train. That was Zhitomir. Zhitomir. Could you spell that for me? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Russian. Z-I-T-O-M-I-R. Zhitomir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you are in the Soviet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you are in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, yeah. And we got on that train, on a train in Zhitomir, and they started-- started to bomb, the Germans. The bombs were flying and we were in the-- it was like cattle cars with no place to-- to sit, just on-- on the-- on the floor. And when the bombs were flying there, people were running through the little windows, you know. It's a freight train. So no big windows to get out. And a lot of them were killed. And I was very lucky. We stayed in that car, train car, with the baby and my husband and another few people, and nothing happened to our car. A lot of them got burned and-- don't ask what was doing there. And we survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going from town to town--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your baby and your husband, what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. We slept in the-- in-- on the grass or in the park, wherever we could. You know, I was two years homeless, completely homeless.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We got-- somebody gave it. We had some things that we could trade yet. I told you, we took some things from home. And from Zhitomir--

speaker: interviewer
text: So at '41, you were how many years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: 24 years old. Your baby is a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you're with your husband. What about your family that you left in Tuchin? What about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was only 50 years old, a beautiful woman. I never saw her again, not my mother, not my father. Nobody. They were all killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From my town, with a baby to escape-- I was the only woman with a baby to escape. The only one. It was-- it was a small town, but there were 4,000 Jews killed in that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans, sure. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Never saw my parents again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Yeah, one sister escaped. She's in New York now-- the youngest, Leia.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking now that you're-- you're in a car and you're hiding with your husband and your baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Zhitomir.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zhitomir.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They fixed the train and we went farther. They didn't take us out. You see, Russia supplied us-- the Soviet Union supplied with-- with trains. We didn't pay for it. They let us go. But they took us to Kiev. Next was Kiev, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this now? Still the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, '41. Went to-- to Kiev. And you couldn't get out of Kiev already because all the Jews were at the train stations and at the boats, because everyone was running away. The Germans were coming very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it took us-- it was terrible in Kiev because we couldn't get on a train or on a-- on-- on-- we tried to get with a boat, with the Dnieper. That's a river, Dnieper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Say that again, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dnieper [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- in '41. And we couldn't get out with that. They wouldn't let us on the boat. So we went with the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Dnieper is what? What is that? What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: That word that you just said, Dnieper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dnieper is a river.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a river.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a big river in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finally got into the train station. It was terrible. I-- they-- There was a-- a fence, and I couldn't go over. My husband went over there and I give him the-- the baby. He had it on the other side to get to the train. And I was on top. And the soldiers, the Russian soldiers, were pulling me from my feet. They wouldn't let me. But I had the baby there, so how could I stay here? So they tore everything I had on me. And I got over.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were trying to keep you from going over the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because there's too many people coming there, not because they didn't like me. Because everybody wanted to go away, especially Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when I got on the other side. And we were able to get away. We waited a few days until we got the train, and we went away. And they took us all the way to Tambow, Tambow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] T-A-M-B-O-W. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by the trains. So I don't know. A few days, I guess, because they stopped every place. It's during a war. You can't do what you want to do there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're asking me-- I don't even know where we got food. We waited until we got something. It was very hard. But when we came to Tambow it-- it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Tambow is in where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not far from Moscow, 200 miles from Moscow. And the people were wonderful there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us food. They let us sleep there in a kolkhoz. You know what's a kolkhoz?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Commune, like. Like a kibbutz in Israel, you know? That's a kolkhoz. They don't own anything. It's the government, but they work and get food and whatever. But they really were the most wonderful people. They tried to give us and help us, but we heard on the radio that the Germans are coming close to Moscow. So how could we stay there? We had to get away. So we were trying to get away to Central Asia. Maybe we'll get to Palestine that way. That was the only way we could think of to get to Palestine at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in kolkhoz-- kolkhoz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolkhoz in Tambow. That's Tambow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two months. Two months, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the baby, you were able to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, with the baby. They were wonderful. They helped me a lot. I'll never forget that. They really did. And from there, I still had a gold watch. So I sold it to the people there, in Tambow. And we bought a ticket-- tickets to go to Tashkent. That's Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uzbekistan, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we got not too far with the train with the tickets. In Kuybyshev-- Kuybyshev, they took us off. They said we had to get another train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who took you off?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the Soviet Union, the people, the whatever. And we had to wait for another train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any particular reason they took you off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the Soviet Union, you don't ask for reasons. They do what they do. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we stayed and waited for a train, but we couldn't get a train. So we got the-- they gave us a train, like a cattle train. And we were sitting there on the train. My baby got sick. It was just terrible until-- and my baby got very sick. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I do? We didn't even-- we had some water. It was cold already. We escaped in June, and that was already the fall. And we put cold compresses on his head, and he had high fever. And we went maybe-- maybe two or three weeks with that train, and he was very, very sick, until we got to Tashkent. In Tashkent, they took him off and they took him to a hospital because it showed already, it was measles. Can imagine. And there, I came to Tashkent. They took him to the hospital. And I was lying by the-- the stairs, the hospital steps. They wouldn't let me in to stay with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent is in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pakistan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uzbekistan, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's something like Pakistan. So he was very, very sick that time. And we stayed in a park. You know, not only us, hundreds of Jews were on that park. Everybody slept outside on the ground. See, everybody was homeless-- the Jews that escaped. They were. And he was a whole month in that hospital. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is all in '41. And from there, when I took him out from the hospital, I signed papers. I took him out because he would have died there anyway. So they sent us to a kolkhoz in Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not a kibbutz. It's a kibbutz-like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like a kolkhoz, but that Muslims. That's not Christian people.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to stop? So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed in that kolkhoz. And that's where I saved my baby. Because the Uzbeks, they were Muslims but they were good people. They helped us a lot. They gave me milk and I could really save my baby there. And that's a place where grapes grow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But everybody was hungry at that time. At that time was terrible hunger. People were dying like flies because there was nothing to eat. They didn't have either. Nobody had. How were Jews--
